evidence_quotes.quote: Developer Verification is, in reality, a euphemism for mandatory self-doxxing.

Moving forward, Google proposes to restrict the installation of any software from any marketplace or developer that has not been registered with Google by, among other things, submitting your government identification.

paragraphs.text: I think that the recent move by Google about the new restrictions to their Android platform is not in-line with the Digital Fairness Act (DFA): According to Google themselves it's "a new layer of security for certified Android devices" called "Developer Verification." Developer Verification is, in reality, a euphemism for mandatory self-doxxing. Moving forward, Google proposes to restrict the installation of any software from any marketplace or developer that has not been registered with Google by, among other things, submitting your government identification. The change is presented as an even-handed attempt to protect all users from the potential harms of malware while preserving the system's openness. Whle on the surface this looks like a company trying to enhance security by preventing the end-users on installing unthrustworty apps, this really should not be part of an open and secure system- in fact, it's really restrictive and not fair for us people who want privacy, control and freedom about how we develop apps (and how we use them); also, Android as a platform has plenty of security measures regarding installing apps from "unknown sources" (or anything that isn't the Google Play Store). Furthermore, this move by Google's part is looking like a ploy to strengthen their grip on a platform, potentially introducing misleading and unfair practices by restricting everyone in their platform. Also as a EU citizen, I'm quite afraid about the potential of this move backfiring and creating problems on a political level, especially because Alphabet (Google's parent company) is very close to the US amministration.
